set_wc() doesn't really do anything. It derefs the pointer, so the value gets copied into the stack. It then sets the flag on the stack value, leaving the original unchanged.

You can confirm the above by placing a trace in, say, drm_shmem_vmap() after a call to set_wc(true). The underlying value in the C struct will still be 0.

Hey y'all! i've been picking back up the gem shmem series and cleaning it up, and one of the things that I've been trying to decide is how to fix what I -think- is a soundness issue with the gem bindings but could also certainly be me misunderstanding something. So: in the series that got sent out there's two specific functions that caught my eye: shmem::Object::set_wc()and shmem::Object::share_dma_resv(). The first one mentions in the comments that it has ordering requirements w/r/t being called before the gem object gets shared (meaning either it needs to be unsafe, or we just expose it with a slightly nicer API that is sound), and the second one -appears- to only really make sense with exclusive object to the gem object aswell.

Fixing this is pretty easy, I'm just not certain which approach we'd rather have here. The first one I tried but am not 100% fond of is having a UniqueObject that gives us access to these methods, where you need to consume the UniqueObject type to a ARef<Object<T>> to start using it. The second one I'm leaning a bit more towards is just getting rid of both functions entirely, and instead allowing users to pass a ObjectConfig to Object::new() that controls whether or not map_wc is set and whether or not we share a different object's dma_resv. Any preferences here from y'all?

on asahi side both are only called directly after object creation so ObjectConfig wouldn't cause issues. Hard to imagine that this would be something to be switched dynamically per object.
Same is true for the added set_exportable() which met resistance against adding it to the C side so we probably will add the driver callback for it
cool - sounds like I'm on the right path then
hm - I also wonder if SGTable and SGEntry should maybe be split out into a different module
yes, it certainly is not DRM/gem specific. maybe rust/kernel/dma.rs or rust/kernel/scatterlist.rs(to follow linux/scatterlist.h)

I think the challenge is figuring out how we would want to keep the object being described alive. Right now SGTable<T> actually holds an ARef<> to the gem object that its using, but I would assume we would want something different for an SGTable<T> that lives outside of the gem shmem crate

actually - I'm thinking about it and I honestly wonder if the correct answer here might be to simply not handle lifetimes with SGTable and define it as an unsafe interface where users are expected to write their own wrappers around it that ensure that whatever memory is being described stays alive for as long as the SGTable. So you'd have like: unsafe fn new(…) -> SGTable and then the gem shmem bindings would implement their own type around SGTable that fulfills the safety contract by holding an ARef<Object<T>>
...also, fun discovery I just made: there's a memory leak here I think. I don't actually see any code here that handles freeing the memory for the sgtable
(also @Danilo Krummrich ^ let me know if you have any thoughts on this)

/// A GEM object whose private-data layout is not known.
///
/// Not all GEM objects are created equal, and subsequently drivers may occasionally need to deal
/// with situations where they are working with a GEM object whose [`DriverObject`] implementation
/// is ambiguous, or simply doesn't exist (e.g. a GEM object created from an ffi call).
///
/// It may be used just like a normal [`Object`], with the exception that it cannot access
/// driver-private data.
///
/// # Invariant
///
/// Via `#[repr(transparent)]`, this type is guaranteed to have an identical data layout to
/// `struct drm_gem_object`.
#[repr(transparent)]
pub struct OpaqueObject<T: drm::Driver>(Opaque<bindings::drm_gem_object>, PhantomData<T>);

impl<T: drm::Driver> IntoGEMObject for OpaqueObject<T> {
    type Driver = T;

    unsafe fn as_ref<'a>(self_ptr: *mut bindings::drm_gem_object) -> &'a Self {
        // SAFETY:
        // - This cast is safe via our type invariant.
        // - `self_ptr` is guaranteed to be a valid pointer to a gem object by our safety contract.
        unsafe { &*self_ptr.cast().cast_const() }
    }

    fn as_gem_obj(&self) -> *mut bindings::drm_gem_object {
        self.0.get()
    }
}

impl<D, T> AsRef<OpaqueObject<D>> for T
where
    T: IntoGEMObject<Driver = D>,
    D: drm::Driver,
{
    fn as_ref(&self) -> &OpaqueObject {
        // SAFETY: This cast is safe via our type invariant.
        unsafe { &*self.as_gem_obj().cast_const() }
    }
}

I came across this line of code:

I'm a bit concerned because AlwaysRefCounted can only have a single blanket implementation globally. Having one for IntoGEMObject means that no other trait can imply a AlwaysRefCounted impl like this.

/// Reads a field of an item from a region of structs within an iosys map.
///
/// # Examples
///
/// Where `map` is an `IoSysMapRef` which contains 3 `Foo`s.
///
/// ```
/// # use kernel::iosys_map::{*, tests::*};
/// ##[derive(Default)]
/// struct Foo {
///     a: u8,
/// };
///
/// // SAFETY: All bit patterns are acceptable values for `Foo`.
/// unsafe impl kernel::transmute::FromBytes for Foo{};
/// // SAFETY: Instances of `Foo` have no uninitialized portions.
/// unsafe impl kernel::transmute::AsBytes for Foo{};
///
/// # let map = VecIoSysMap::<Foo>::new(3).unwrap();
/// let whole = kernel::iosys_map_read!(map[2]);
/// let field = kernel::iosys_map_read!(map[1].a);
/// ```
#[macro_export]
macro_rules! iosys_map_read {
    ($map:expr, $idx:expr, $($field:tt)*) => {{
        (|| -> ::core::result::Result<_, $crate::error::Error> {
            let base_offset = $crate::iosys_map::IoSysMapRef::item_from_index(&$map, $idx)?;

            // SAFETY: `base_offset` came from item_from_index(), which always returns a valid
            // offset within the iosys map.
            let base_ptr = unsafe {
                $crate::iosys_map::IoSysMapRef::ptr_from_offset(&$map, base_offset)
            };

            // SAFETY: `ptr_from_index()` always returns a valid pointer to an item within the
            // memory region pointed to by the iosys mapping.
            let field_ptr = unsafe { &raw const (*base_ptr) $($field)* };

            // SAFETY: field_ptr points to a field contained within the struct pointed to by
            // item_ptr, so we know that field_ptr is always located after item_ptr and thus this
            // subtraction will not underflow.
            let field_offset = unsafe { field_ptr.byte_offset_from(base_ptr) } as usize;

            // SAFETY:
            // - Both field_ptr and item_ptr are valid pointers in the iosys map, and contained
            //   within the same allocation.
            // - `base_offset` is derived from item_from_index(), which always returns a valid
            //   offset within the iosys map.
            // - Since we derived `field_offset` from `field_ptr - item_ptr`, we know `field_offset`
            //   does not go out of the bounds of a single item within the iosys map.
            // - Thus, `base_offset + field_offset` are guaranteed to refer to a valid offset within
            //   the iosys_map that corresponds to the field we are trying to write to.
            // - Additionally, since the resulting offset will be valid we are guaranteed that this
            //   addition will not overflow.
            unsafe {
                let offset = base_offset.unchecked_add(field_offset);

                // (confirm that we're correct and field_offset truly does not go out of bounds)
                debug_assert!(offset < $map.size());

                ::core::result::Result::Ok(
                    $crate::iosys_map::IoSysMapRef::field_read(&$map, offset)
                )
            }
        })()
    }};
    ($map:ident [ $idx:expr ] $($field:tt)*) => {
        $crate::iosys_map_read!($map, $idx, $($field)*)
    };
    ($($map:ident).* [ $idx:expr ] $($field:tt)*) => {
        $crate::iosys_map_read!($($map).*, $idx, $($field)*)
    };
}

        // SAFETY: If the offset is out of bounds, we'll catch this via overflow checks or when
        // checking range_end.
        let offset = unsafe { self.item_from_index(offset)? };
        let range_size = count.checked_mul(self.item_size()).ok_or(EOVERFLOW)?;
        let range_end = offset.checked_add(range_size).ok_or(EOVERFLOW)?;

It's a bit of a new pattern but we do it for Kms with some fancy trait tricks:

  • drm::Driver has associated type Kms which requires KmsImplbe implemented for the type that is passed
  • KmsDriver is the trait a driver actually implements for KMS support which provides an automatic KmsImpl implementation. PhantomData (will replace with a dedicated type next respin) also implements KmsImpl and can be passed instead of a KmsDriver implementor for drivers that don't have KMS
  • all of this brings me to how we actually determine the feature flags. KmsImpl has a Optional able of the C vtable functions that is None on stubbed implementations like PhantomData, so we can check whether it's none or some at compile time to figure out whether we want to add the feature flags or not

BTW - I saw those concurrency issues get pointed out as well and I'm not totally sure they're correct, but it seemed to go a bit more into Devres then I've dug before so there could be something I just didn't understand

But it seemed like the concurrency issue it was talking about implied that there could somehow be a race between driver unbinding and the destructor for Devres, which, feels like it doesn't make sense as I would have assumed the whole point of Devres is to provide synchronization around that

So for normal Devres resources, it is fine to rely on the synchronization provided by Devres. You just need this to be cleaned up, and you just need to avoid UAF when accessing it, and Devres provides that.

However in this case, as Sashiko pointed out, you're actually relying on the fact that, when Devres::drop returns, the destructor has already been executed. Unfortunately this is not a guarantee that Devres provides, because the drop can occur asynchronously in the unbind thread.
Indeed, I suggest we make this a note in the Devres documentation, as it be easily overlooked.
@Lyude Do you want to send a patch for this?

In particular, the guarantee is similar to the one the C API (such as devres_release()) provides: Devres::drop is only guaranteed to have the destructor of T called if it is called from a Bound context.
It relaxes the C API however, as devres_release() is not valid to be called outside of a Bound context in general.
